intro:
Planning a fence project requires knowing exactly how many panels and posts you need before visiting the lumber yard. Our fence calculator computes the perimeter of your yard, then divides by your panel length to determine panels needed and adds one post per panel plus corner posts for total post count.

The standard fence panel is 8 feet long in the US, though 6-foot panels are also common. Posts are typically set every 8 feet for wood privacy fences. Gates require two posts each for proper frame support. Plan for gates in the perimeter measurement — a 4-foot gate replaces one 4-foot section of fencing.

Always call 811 (US Dig Safe hotline) before any fence installation to locate underground utility lines. Most jurisdictions also require a permit for fences over 6 feet — check local codes.
howItWorks: Enter your yard's length and width in feet, panel length in feet (default 8 ft), and number of gates. The calculator computes perimeter, divides by panel length for panels needed, and computes posts needed (number of panels plus one end post, plus 2 extra posts per gate).
interpretationGuide: Panels needed is for the fence sections only, not gate panels. Posts needed includes line posts, corner posts, and gate posts. Add 10–15% extra lumber for waste and cuts. Purchase posts at post length plus 2 feet (for below-grade anchoring).

faqs.question: How deep should fence posts be set?
faqs.answer: The general rule is to bury at least one-third of the post's total length. For a 6-foot fence, use 9-foot posts set 3 feet deep. For a 4-foot fence, 6-foot posts set 2 feet deep. Below the frost line in cold climates is essential to prevent heaving.

faqs.question: What is the standard fence post spacing?
faqs.answer: Standard spacing for 8-foot fence panels is 8 feet on center (center of one post to center of next). For 6-foot panels, space posts 6 feet apart. Posts should be set every 6–8 feet for structural integrity, regardless of panel size.

faqs.question: Should I use wood or metal posts?
faqs.answer: Pressure-treated wood posts (4×4 or 6×6) are most common for wood fences. Cedar and redwood resist rot naturally. Metal posts (galvanized steel) last longer and resist rot but cost more. For chain-link and metal fences, steel posts are standard. Wood posts should be rated for ground contact (UC4B or UC4C).

faqs.question: What permits do I need for a fence?
faqs.answer: Most municipalities require permits for fences over 6 feet, fences near property lines, or fences in front yards. Check local zoning regulations — many have specific rules about fence height, materials, and setback from property lines. HOAs may have additional restrictions.

faqs.question: How much does fence installation cost?
faqs.answer: DIY material costs: $8–$20 per linear foot for wood privacy fence, $15–$35 for vinyl, $10–$15 for chain-link. Professional installation: $20–$60 per linear foot for wood, $30–$70 for vinyl, $15–$30 for chain-link. Gate installation adds $200–$600 per gate depending on size and material.

faqs.question: What is the best wood for fence panels?
faqs.answer: Pressure-treated pine: most affordable, rot-resistant, widely available, requires staining/sealing. Cedar: naturally rot-resistant, lightweight, takes stain well, premium cost. Redwood: premium rot resistance, attractive grain, most expensive. Composite: low maintenance, 20+ year lifespan, higher upfront cost.

faqs.question: How do I account for gate openings in my fence calculation?
faqs.answer: This calculator accounts for gates by adding 2 extra posts per gate. Gate openings do not need fence panels — instead, you buy gate hardware and gate frame lumber. Standard single gate is 4 feet wide; double drive gate is 10–12 feet wide.

faqs.question: How far from the property line can I build a fence?
faqs.answer: Typical setback requirements are 2–6 feet from the property line for side and rear fences. Front yard fences often have stricter setbacks (6–10 feet from sidewalk). Always confirm exact requirements with your local building department and verify the actual property line location — don't assume the existing fence or neighbor's assumption is correct.

faqs.question: How long does a wood fence last?
faqs.answer: Properly installed and maintained wood fences last 10–20 years. Pressure-treated pine: 15–25 years. Cedar: 15–30 years with proper maintenance. Key factors: post depth, drainage at base, regular staining/sealing every 2–3 years, and keeping soil away from the base.

faqs.question: What is split-rail vs. privacy fence?
faqs.answer: Split-rail (ranch-style) fences use 2–3 horizontal rails between posts with large gaps — decorative and good for livestock but not private. Privacy fences have vertical boards (pickets) installed tightly or with minimal gaps for complete visual screening. Shadowbox fences alternate boards on each side for privacy with airflow.
